Velocity and Acceleration Vectors in Uniform Circular Motion: A point mass on a circle with continually updating tangent velocity and inward centripetal acceleration vectors.
Origin of Centripetal Force: Direction Change: Illustrating how an inward force continuously redirects the velocity vector, preventing the object from moving in a straight line.
Vehicle on Level Road: Top-Down Force Diagram: A car model from above, showing its velocity vector and the static friction force vector pointing towards the center of the turn.
Vehicle on Level Road: Front-View Force Diagram: A car model from the front, displaying gravity (down), normal force (up), and the horizontal static friction force (inward).
Free Body Diagram of Car on Curve: Isolating the car as a point mass, showing only the static friction force as the sole horizontal force providing centripetal acceleration.
Centripetal Force Equation for Friction: Animating the derivation and application of F_friction = mv^2/r, with labels for each variable.
Derivation of Maximum Safe Speed: Step-by-step animation of "mu_s N = mv_max^2/r", substituting N=mg, leading to "v_max = sqrt(mu_s g r)".
Visualizing Skidding Due to Excessive Speed: A car path initially following the circle, then deviating outwards tangentially when speed exceeds v_max and friction fails.
Impact of Turn Radius on Maximum Speed: Two identical cars on separate circular paths with different radii, demonstrating how larger radius allows higher v_max for the same friction.

Impact of Friction Coefficient on Maximum Speed: A car on a circular path, demonstrating how changing the road surface (e.g., dry to wet) changes 
"mu_s" and thus v_max, leading to skidding if speed is maintained.
Inertia's Role in Circular Motion: Showing an object "trying" to move straight due to inertia, while the centripetal force continuously "pulls" it into the curve.
Forces in Equilibrium (Vertical) vs. Unbalanced (Horizontal): Clearly separating the balanced vertical forces (N=mg) from the unbalanced horizontal force (friction = Fc).

Path Comparison: Straight vs. Circular: A car moving linearly vs. a car turning, highlighting the constant velocity in linear motion versus the constantly changing velocity direction in circular motion.
Tire-Road Interaction for Friction: Zooming into the tire contact patch, illustrating the microscopic forces that constitute static friction providing the centripetal force.
Effect of Mass on Maximum Speed: Animating that the maximum safe speed is independent of the vehicle's mass (m cancels out), showing two cars of different masses turning at the same v_max.
Equilibrium Position Visualization: A spring at rest, highlighting its natural length and zero-displacement point.

Spring Extension Animation: A spring being slowly extended by an external force, showing the displacement 'x' and the increasing restoring force.
Force Vector Visualization: Displaying the restoring force vector (F = -kx) acting on the spring as it's deformed, dynamically changing in magnitude and direction.
Work Done by External Force: Animating the area under the Force-Displacement (F vs x) graph as the spring is deformed, representing the work input.
Potential Energy Formula Introduction: Visually deriving and constructing the U = 0.5kx^2 formula by integrating the force-displacement graph.

Visualizing Force as a Push/Pull Vector: An object being pushed or pulled, with an animated force arrow emerging from the point of contact, showing magnitude and direction.

Mass as Inertia: Resistance to Acceleration: Two objects of visibly different masses (e.g., a small box and a large crate). Apply the same animated force arrow to each, showing the lighter object accelerating more rapidly.
Acceleration as the Rate of Change of Velocity: An object moving, displaying its instantaneous velocity vector as it speeds up, slows down, or changes direction.
Introducing the Equation F=ma: The equation F=ma appears on screen, with F, m, and a highlighted sequentially as their definitions are briefly shown.

Direct Proportionality: Force vs. Acceleration (Constant Mass): A fixed-mass object. Show a small force arrow causing a small acceleration arrow, then double the force arrow, and observe the acceleration arrow also doubling.

Inverse Proportionality: Mass vs. Acceleration (Constant Force): A constant force arrow applied to two objects of different masses. Show the lighter object experiencing greater acceleration than the heavier one.
Net Force: Summing Collinear Forces: An object with two opposing force arrows applied. Show the vector addition/subtraction resulting in a single net force arrow and subsequent acceleration.
Net Force: Summing Perpendicular Forces: An object with two perpendicular force arrows. Illustrate the parallelogram rule to find the resultant net force vector and the direction of acceleration.

Newton's Second Law with Friction (Horizontal Surface): A block being pushed horizontally. Display the applied force, the opposing kinetic friction force, the resulting net force, and the block's acceleration.
Free-Body Diagrams and F=ma for a Stationary Object: A book resting on a table. Draw and label the gravitational force and the normal force, showing they cancel out (Net F = 0, so a = 0).

Applying F=ma to a Vertically Thrown Object (After Release): An object thrown upwards. Show only the constant downward gravitational force (ignoring air resistance), and the resulting constant downward acceleration (g).

Block Sliding Down an Inclined Plane (No Friction): A block on a ramp. Decompose the gravitational force into components parallel and perpendicular to the incline, showing the net force causing acceleration down the ramp.
Tension in a Rope: Pulling a Block: A block being pulled by a rope. Show the tension force acting along the rope and calculate the acceleration using F=ma.

Centripetal Force in Uniform Circular Motion: An object moving in a circle at constant speed. Illustrate the centripetal force vector always pointing towards the center of the circle, causing constant change in velocity direction.

Determining Mass from Force and Acceleration Measurements: Given an animated force vector and resulting acceleration vector (e.g., from motion data), dynamically calculate and display the object's mass using m=F/a.
Vector Representation of Forces: Animating force vectors (arrows) originating from a single point, showing magnitude (length) and direction.
Head-to-Tail Vector Sum for Equilibrium: Demonstrating three or more concurrent force vectors adding up graphically to form a closed polygon, illustrating zero net force.
Resolution of a Single Force: Animating the decomposition of an angled force vector into its horizontal (x) and vertical (y) components.
Analytical Equilibrium Conditions (ΣFx=0, ΣFy=0): Visualizing how the sum of all x-components and all y-components of concurrent forces cancel out independently.
Equilibrium of a Mass Suspended by Two Strings: Animating the tension forces in two strings and the gravitational force acting on a suspended mass, showing their vector balance.
Forces on a Block on an Inclined Plane (Static): Illustrating the normal force, static friction, gravitational force, and an external pulling/pushing force acting on a block at rest on an incline.
Lami's Theorem Application: Animating three concurrent forces in equilibrium, showing the relationship between each force's magnitude and the sine of the angle between the other two forces.
Force Table Experiment Simulation: Visualizing three or more weights creating forces that balance a central ring, with vectors dynamically adjusting.
Equilibrium of a Traffic Light Suspended by Three Cables: Showing the tension forces in three cables and the gravitational force balancing at the traffic light's suspension point.
A Crate Pulled by Two Ropes on a Smooth Surface: Animating two tension forces and the normal/gravity forces balancing, resulting in horizontal equilibrium.
Forces on a Kite in Steady Flight: Visualizing lift, drag, gravity, and the tension in the string acting concurrently on a kite in equilibrium.
Varying Suspension Angles: Animating how the magnitudes of tension forces in two wires change as their angles relative to the horizontal (or vertical) are adjusted while supporting a constant mass.
Equilibrium of a Simple Pendulum at Rest: Showing the tension force and gravitational force acting on the pendulum bob, demonstrating their direct opposition.
Pushing a Block Against a Wall with an Angled Force: Visualizing the applied force, normal force from the wall, static friction, and gravity balancing to hold the block in place.
Balancing a Mobile (Simplified): A single point where multiple elements (weights, other parts of the mobile) exert forces, showing their concurrent equilibrium.
Fundamental Dimensions as Geometric Primitives: Visualizing Length, Mass, Time as independent axes or blocks.
Building Derived Dimensions: Animating the formation of Area (L^2) from Length, and Volume (L^3) from Area.
Velocity: Distance per Unit Time: Illustrating motion and the division of Length by Time (L T^-1).
Acceleration: Rate of Change of Velocity: Showing velocity changing over time, leading to L T^-2.
Force: The Product of Mass and Acceleration: Animating a mass being accelerated, combining M and L T^-2 to form M L T^-2.
Work and Energy: Force Times Distance: Visualizing a force moving an object over a distance, deriving M L^2 T^-2.
Power: Work Done per Unit Time: Animating the rate at which work is performed, resulting in M L^2 T^-3.
Dimensional Homogeneity: The Addition Rule: Demonstrating that only quantities with the same dimensions can be added or subtracted.
Checking Equations with Dimensional Analysis: Verifying the consistency of an equation like v = u + at by comparing dimensions of each term.
Deriving Dimensions for Universal Gravitational Constant (G): Visually solving for the dimensions of G from Newton's Law of Gravitation (M^-1 L^3 T^-2).
Dimensionless Quantities: Angle and Strain: Illustrating how ratios of quantities with the same dimension result in dimensionless values.
Limitations: The Role of Dimensionless Constants: Showing how dimensional analysis cannot determine numerical constants like 1/2 in kinetic energy.
Dimensions in Electromagnetism: Charge and Current: Visualizing the relationship between charge (Q) and current (I) over time (T), Q = IT.

Conservation of Angular Momentum and Radius of Gyration: Illustrating how k changes during rotation (e.g., ice skater).
Angular Momentum Definition (L = r x p): Visualizing position (r) and momentum (p) vectors for a point mass, and the resulting angular momentum vector (L) using the right-hand rule.
Angular Momentum of a Point Mass in Circular Motion: A particle moving in a circle, showing constant angular momentum vector about the center.
Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body (L = I  ): A rotating disk or cylinder, illustrating the relationship between moment of inertia, angular velocity vector, and angular momentum vector.
Conservation of Angular Momentum - Ice Skater: An animated figure pulling in arms while spinning, showing increase in angular velocity due to decrease in moment of inertia.
Conservation of Angular Momentum - Planetary Orbit: A planet orbiting a star under a central force, demonstrating the constant angular momentum vector and constant areal velocity.
Torque and Change in Angular Momentum (   = dL/dt): Applying a force (creating torque) on a spinning object, showing how the angular momentum vector changes magnitude and/or direction.
Gyroscopic Precession: A spinning top or gyroscope precessing under gravity, clearly showing the angular momentum vector, torque vector, and the resulting precession.
Nutational Motion of a Gyroscope: Visualizing the oscillatory 'wobble' (nutation) superimposed on the steady precession of a gyroscope.
Angular Momentum in Rotational Collisions: Two initially rotating or stationary disks undergoing an inelastic collision and coupling, illustrating the conservation of total angular momentum.
Angular Momentum of Rolling Motion: A wheel rolling without slipping, demonstrating the total angular momentum about its center of mass and about the instantaneous point of contact.
Vector Nature of Angular Momentum: Animated demonstration of the right-hand rule to determine the direction of angular velocity and angular momentum vectors for various rotations.
Angular Momentum and Rotational Stability: A spinning bicycle wheel demonstrating its resistance to tipping over compared to a stationary wheel.
Angular Momentum of a System of Particles: Visualizing multiple point masses rotating around a common axis, showing the vector sum of individual angular momenta.

Effect of Moment of Inertia on Angular Momentum: Comparing two objects with different mass distributions (e.g., a solid disk vs. a ring) rotating at the same angular velocity, highlighting their different angular momentum magnitudes.
Angular Impulse (J_L =   L): An animation showing a short-duration torque applied to an object, resulting in a quantifiable change in its angular momentum.
Two blocks of different masses colliding and sticking together in one dimension
A bullet embedding into a stationary wooden block, with the combined system moving
Visualizing kinetic energy loss as heat and deformation during a collision using bar graphs
Momentum conservation shown with vector arrows for each object before and after collision
Tracking the constant velocity of the center of mass for a two-object system during the collision
Collision of two cars showing visible crumpling and deformation as they stick together
Graphing individual object velocities over time, showing the instantaneous change and common final velocity
Visualizing the impulse exerted on each object during the collision using a force-time graph
Comparing scenarios with varying initial velocities (fixed masses) to show different final velocities and energy losses
A pile driver striking a post, showing the combined system moving into the ground
A ball of clay hitting a target and visibly deforming as it sticks
A block colliding with and sticking to an effectively immovable wall, demonstrating complete kinetic energy loss
Explicitly showing the relative velocity of objects becoming zero after a perfectly inelastic collision

Visualizing the perpendicular distance (lever arm): A force vector applied at a point on a rigid body, rotating about a pivot, showing the construction of the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force.

Vector Cross Product r x F: Animation of position vector r from pivot to point of force application, force vector F, and the resulting torque vector tau emerging from the pivot, demonstrating the right-hand rule.
Effect of Angle on Torque: A fixed force F applied at a fixed distance r, but with varying angles theta between r and F, showing how tau = r F sin(theta) changes from maximum to zero.
Changing the Pivot Point: A rigid rod with a constant force F applied. Animation showing how the torque changes as the pivot point O is moved along the rod.

Static Equilibrium of a Beam: A horizontal beam supported at two points, with multiple downward forces (weights) applied. Animation demonstrating that the sum of clockwise torques equals the sum of counter-clockwise torques for equilibrium.
Torque of a Couple: Two equal and opposite forces F and -F applied at two different points on a rigid body, showing the net torque being independent of the pivot point and zero net force.
Wrench Turning a Bolt: A wrench handle, a bolt (pivot), and a hand applying force at the end of the wrench, showing the rotational motion of the bolt.

Door Opening Mechanism: A door hinged on one side. Animation showing forces applied at different distances from the hinge, illustrating that a force applied further from the hinge produces more torque.
Balancing a Seesaw: Two objects of different masses placed at different distances from the fulcrum of a seesaw, showing the fulcrum adjusting to balance (or the seesaw tilting if unbalanced).
Torque and Angular Acceleration: A disk rotating about its center. Animation showing a tangential force F causing an angular acceleration alpha, demonstrating tau = I * alpha qualitatively.
Torque on a Bicycle Pedal: A simplified bicycle pedal and crank arm system. Animation showing the force from the foot on the pedal creating torque about the crank's pivot point.

Gravity's Torque on a Pendulum: A simple pendulum swinging. Animation showing the gravitational force mg acting on the bob and its component creating torque about the pivot point, causing oscillation.
Torque in a Gear System: Two meshing gears of different radii. Animation showing how a torque applied to the input gear is transmitted and transformed (speed vs. torque) to the output gear.
Torque Caused by Tension: A string wrapped around a pulley, with a weight hanging from it. Animation showing the tension in the string creating torque on the pulley, causing it to rotate.
Torque on a Diving Board: A person standing at the end of a diving board. Animation showing the weight of the person creating a torque about the fixed end of the board, causing it to bend (deflect).
Introduction to Moment of Inertia: Visualizing a point mass rotating around an axis with varying distance.
The Parallel Axis Theorem Formula: Animated display of I = I_cm + Md^2 with labels and component highlight.
Geometric Interpretation: Dynamic illustration of a 2D shape, its center of mass, and an arbitrary parallel axis.
Derivation for a System of Point Masses: Step-by-step algebraic and geometric animation of the PAT derivation.
Differential Mass Element Integration Concept: Visualizing 'dm' and its contribution to the integral for a continuous body.
Application: Thin Rod - Axis Through Center of Mass: Calculation of I_cm and its animated rotation.
Application: Thin Rod - Axis Through One End: Applying PAT to find I for a rod rotating about its end, highlighting 'd'.
Application: Rectangular Plate - Axis Through Center: Visualizing the plate rotating about its CM axis and its I_cm calculation.
Application: Rectangular Plate - Axis Through a Corner: Using PAT to determine I for a rectangular plate rotating about a corner.
Application: Solid Disk - Axis Through Center: Animated calculation and rotation of a disk about its central axis.
Application: Solid Disk - Axis Tangential to Edge: Applying PAT to find I for a disk rotating about an axis tangent to its circumference.
Composite Shape Example: L-shaped object's Moment of Inertia about an external axis using PAT.
Rolling Cylinder/Sphere: Decomposing kinetic energy into translational and rotational, showing where PAT applies for the rotational part.
Physical Pendulum Period: Demonstrating how PAT is crucial for calculating the period of an extended object pendulum.
Rotational Dynamics Comparison: Animating two identical objects, one with small 'd' and one with large 'd', showing different angular accelerations under the same torque.
Initial setup: Displaying two point masses (M1, M2) at specific 1D coordinates with their respective mass labels.
Defining the coordinate system: Placing an origin and positive X-axis, showing position vectors r1 and r2 for M1 and M2.
Formula introduction: Displaying the center of mass formula X_CoM = (M1*X1 + M2*X2) / (M1 + M2).
Step-by-step calculation: Animating the substitution of numerical values into the formula and showing the intermediate calculation steps.
Locating the CoM: Placing a distinct marker (e.g., a blinking star) at the calculated X_CoM position on the line.
Varying M1's mass: Gradually increasing M1's mass (e.g., making its circle grow) and dynamically updating the CoM's position, showing it shift towards M1.
Varying M2's mass: Gradually increasing M2's mass and dynamically updating the CoM's position, showing it shift towards M2.
Equal masses scenario: Setting M1 = M2 and explicitly showing the CoM settling exactly at the midpoint between the two particles.
Translating M1: Animating M1 smoothly sliding along the X-axis and simultaneously showing the CoM's proportional translation.
Translating M2: Animating M2 smoothly sliding along the X-axis and simultaneously showing the CoM's proportional translation.
Both particles moving towards/away: Animating M1 and M2 moving with constant but different velocities, and tracing the path of the CoM.

Internal forces & CoM: Showing M1 and M2 attracting each other (e.g., with a spring or gravitational force), but the CoM remaining stationary (if initially at rest) or continuing its constant velocity path.
Rotation around CoM (2D): Animating M1 and M2 orbiting their common CoM in a circular path in a 2D plane, with the CoM remaining fixed at the origin.
CoM as the balance point: Visualizing a massless rod connecting M1 and M2, and showing it perfectly balanced when supported at the CoM.
Vector sum representation: Illustrating the CoM position vector as a weighted average of the individual position vectors (r_CoM = (M1*r1 + M2*r2) / (M1 + M2)) with vector addition.
Defining Angular Position and Displacement: A point P on a circle, showing its initial and final angular positions and the arc length traversed.
Instantaneous Angular Velocity Vector: A rotating rigid body illustrating the angular velocity vector along the axis of rotation and its magnitude.
Angular Acceleration from Changing Angular Velocity: A spinning disk speeding up or slowing down, showing the initial and final angular velocity vectors and the resulting angular acceleration.
Relationship between Tangential and Angular Velocity: A point on the rim of a rotating disk, simultaneously showing its tangential velocity vector and the disk's angular velocity (v = r * omega).
Moment of Inertia: Impact of Mass Distribution: Comparing the rotational difficulty of a solid disk versus a ring of the same mass and radius.
Torque as Force x Lever Arm: A wrench turning a bolt, explicitly showing the force vector, the pivot point, and the perpendicular distance (lever arm).
Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body: A spinning flywheel, illustrating its moment of inertia and angular velocity, and the resulting angular momentum vector L = I * omega.
Conservation of Angular Momentum: Rotating Platform: A person on a frictionless rotating platform extending and retracting weights, showing the change in angular speed.
Rotational Kinetic Energy of a Rolling Object: A wheel rolling down an incline, separating its total kinetic energy into translational and rotational components.
Parallel Axis Theorem Visualization: A rectangular plate rotating first about its center of mass, then about an edge, visually demonstrating I_new = I_CM + M*d^2.
Rolling Without Slipping: Velocity Profile: A rolling wheel, showing the velocity vectors of points on its rim: zero at the bottom, 2v_CM at the top.
Centripetal Force in Circular Motion: A satellite orbiting Earth, showing the gravitational force acting as the centripetal force, keeping it in orbit.
Precession of a Top: A spinning top whose axis slowly rotates about the vertical, showing the weight vector, the pivot, the torque vector, and the angular momentum vector.
Work Done by Torque: A motor applying a constant torque to a shaft, showing the angular displacement and the resulting work done (Tau * delta_theta).
Angular Impulse and Change in Angular Momentum: A quick impulse (e.g., a hammer strike) applied to a rotating object, showing the sudden change in its angular momentum.
Defining gravitational potential energy (GPE) for a point mass near Earth's surface as mgh, with visual representation of m, g, and h.
Deriving GPE = mgh by calculating the work done against gravity to lift an object to height h.
Illustrating the arbitrary nature of the 'zero' reference level for GPE using multiple ground planes.
Demonstrating the path independence of GPE by comparing lifting an object vertically versus pushing it up an incline.
Visualizing the transformation of GPE to kinetic energy as a ball falls, with dynamic energy bar graphs.

Mapping GPE changes along a roller coaster track, highlighting maxima and minima at different elevations.

Graphing GPE linearly against height (mgh vs h) for objects close to Earth's surface.
Plotting GPE as an inverse relationship (-GMm/r vs r) for objects far from a central mass, showing the asymptotic behavior.

Calculating the total GPE for a system of three point masses arranged in a triangle configuration.
Illustrating the orbital GPE of a satellite around a planet, showing its relation to orbital radius.
Explaining the concept of 'escape velocity' by showing an object overcoming its gravitational potential well.
Comparing the GPE of an identical object on Earth versus on the Moon, highlighting the difference in gravitational acceleration.
Visualization of Angular Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration Vectors: A point moving on a circle, showing the "Δθ", "ω", and "α" vectors originating from the center of rotation.
Demonstration of Moment of Inertia for a Point Mass and a System of Masses: Showing a single mass rotating, then multiple masses at different radii, calculating "I".
Animation of Parallel Axis Theorem: A rod rotating about its center, then about an end, visually demonstrating the shift in axis and the change in moment of inertia (I_P = I_CM + Md^2).
Torque as a Cross Product (τ = r x F): Illustrating the position vector 'r', force vector 'F', and the resulting torque vector 'τ' with their directions and magnitudes.
Newton's Second Law for Rotation (τ = Iα): Applying a constant torque to a disk, showing increasing angular velocity and constant angular acceleration.

Derivation and Visualization of Rotational Kinetic Energy (K_rot = 1/2 Iω^2): Showing particles within a rotating rigid body, their individual linear kinetic energies, and summing them to arrive at the total rotational kinetic energy.
Work Done by a Constant Torque: An object rotating through an angle, showing the work W = τΔθ and its effect on rotational kinetic energy.
Angular Momentum (L = Iω) and its Vector Direction: A spinning object (e.g., a disk), showing the angular velocity vector and the resulting angular momentum vector.

Conservation of Angular Momentum: Ice Skater Analogy: A figure representing an ice skater, initially spinning slowly with arms outstretched, then pulling arms in and speeding up, demonstrating L_initial = L_final.

Rolling without Slipping: Velocity Analysis: A wheel rolling on a surface, showing the instantaneous velocity vectors of points on its circumference (top, bottom, center) and the point of contact having zero velocity.
Kinematic Equations for Rotational Motion: Analogous to linear equations (ω_f = ω_i + αt, Δθ = ω_i t + 1/2 αt^2), visualized with a rotating object undergoing constant angular acceleration.
Precession of a Gyroscope: A spinning top with its axis tilted, showing the gravitational force creating a torque, and the resulting slow precession of the axis around the vertical.
Combined Translational and Rotational Motion: A sphere rolling down an inclined plane, showing the simultaneous linear acceleration of its center of mass and its angular acceleration.

Principal Axes of Rotation and Moment of Inertia Tensor (Conceptual): Showing an irregular 3D object and how it rotates "smoothly" about specific axes (principal axes) versus wobbling about others (conceptual visualization without tensor math).
Angular Impulse and Change in Angular Momentum (ΔL = ∫τ dt): A brief, strong torque applied to a stationary object, causing it to spin, illustrating the impulse-momentum theorem for rotation.

Statement of Perpendicular Axis Theorem: Visually presenting the formula I_z = I_x + I_y, highlighting the relationship between the moments of inertia.
Derivation: Infinitesimal Mass (dm) Contribution: Zooming into a small 'dm' on the lamina, showing its (x, y) coordinates and its distances from the X, Y, and Z axes.
Derivation: Integration Step: Animating the summation of dm * (distance)^2 over the entire lamina for I_x, I_y, and I_z, leading to the theorem's proof.
Application: Moment of Inertia of a Circular Disc: Calculating I_z for a uniform circular disc about its center, using I_x and I_y about diameter axes.
Application: Moment of Inertia of a Rectangular Plate: Determining I_z for a uniform rectangular plate about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its center.
Application: Moment of Inertia of a Square Lamina: A specific case of the rectangular plate, highlighting the symmetry where I_x = I_y due to equal sides.
Application: Moment of Inertia of an Annular Ring: Calculating I_z for a uniform annular ring about its central axis perpendicular to its plane.
Validity Condition: Planar Body: Demonstrating why the theorem fails for a 3D object (e.g., a cube) by attempting to apply it, showing invalid distances.
Combined Application: Disc with Offset Axis: Using PAT to find I_z about the center, then applying the Parallel Axis Theorem to find I_z about an axis offset from the center.
Rotational Inertia of a Wheel: Visualizing how spoke and rim mass distribution contribute to a wheel's moment of inertia, simplified using components.
Balancing a Rotating Object: Illustrating how uniform mass distribution, understood through moments of inertia, is crucial for stable rotation in machinery.
Conceptualizing Angular Momentum Conservation: Showing how changes in an object's shape (and thus its moment of inertia) affect angular velocity, like a figure skater pulling in arms.

Equilibrium State of a Spring: A spring at its natural length with no external forces, showing zero displacement and internal molecular balance.

Stretching a Spring and Force Vectors: An external force gradually stretching a spring, simultaneously visualizing the external force vector and the opposing restoring force vector (F_ext = kx, F_rest = -kx).
Compressing a Spring and Force Vectors: An external force gradually compressing a spring, showing the external force and the restoring force vectors acting to push it back.
Force-Extension Graph Construction: Animating the plotting of Force vs. Extension points as a spring is stretched and compressed, drawing the linear fit and labeling the slope 'k'.
Comparing Stiff vs. Soft Springs: Two springs side-by-side, one visibly stiffer (larger k), showing different extensions for the same applied force, and their distinct F-x graphs.
Elastic Potential Energy Accumulation: Animating the area under the F-x graph filling up as the spring is stretched, representing the stored potential energy (1/2 kx^2).
Energy Transformation in a Mass-Spring System: A mass oscillating on a spring, showing the real-time conversion between kinetic energy and elastic potential energy with a dynamic bar graph.
Varying Mass in SHM: Two identical springs, one with a small mass and one with a large mass, oscillating simultaneously to visually compare their different periods.
Varying Spring Constant in SHM: Two identical masses, one on a stiff spring and one on a soft spring, oscillating simultaneously to visually compare their different periods.
Springs in Series Connection: Two springs connected end-to-end, showing the total extension and deriving the effective spring constant (1/k_eff = 1/k1 + 1/k2).
Springs in Parallel Connection: Two springs connected side-by-side, sharing a load, showing the total force and deriving the effective spring constant (k_eff = k1 + k2).
Limit of Proportionality and Elastic Limit: A spring being stretched beyond its linear region, then beyond its elastic limit, demonstrating permanent deformation.
Work Done by External Force: A force pulling a spring, showing the work done as the integral of F dx, represented by the accumulating area under the F-x graph.
Damped Oscillation: A mass-spring system oscillating, gradually losing amplitude over time due to a visible damping force (e.g., air resistance or friction).

Hooke's Law with Gravity: A spring hanging vertically, first extending due to its own weight, then showing the additional extension and new equilibrium position upon adding a mass, balancing gravity and spring force.

Definition via Volume Change: An animated cube of material showing uniform pressure application from all sides, leading to a visible, proportional volume reduction, with the formula K = -V(dP/dV) appearing dynamically.

Free Expansion of Gas: A chamber with a removable barrier. Gas particles (e.g., green dots) initially confined to one half, then rapidly dispersing into the empty other half when the barrier is removed, illustrating increased volume and disorder.

Mixing of Two Gases: Two distinct groups of gas particles (e.g., red and blue dots) separated by a barrier. Barrier removed, particles randomly intermingling until uniformly distributed throughout the container.

Frictional Heating: A block sliding on a surface. Zoom-in view showing microscopic irregularities at the interface, particles at the surfaces vibrating more intensely (heating up) due to friction, converting kinetic energy to thermal energy.

Combustion Reaction Visualization: Methane (CH4) and Oxygen (O2) molecules reacting, showing bonds breaking and forming CO2 and H2O, accompanied by a visual burst of energy (light/heat) and an increase in molecular disorder.

Irreversible Piston Expansion: A piston expanding rapidly, showing non-uniform pressure zones and shockwaves within the gas, contrasting with a slow, quasi-static expansion which would be more reversible.

Joule Heating in a Resistor: A wire segment highlighted as a resistor. Electrons (small blue dots) flowing through it, colliding with fixed lattice atoms (larger grey spheres), causing the lattice atoms to vibrate more vigorously (representing heat generation), and the wire changing color to red.
Ink Diffusion in Water: A concentrated blob of dark blue ink particles slowly spreading out and mixing with transparent water particles in a beaker until the solution is uniformly light blue.

Melting Ice Cube: A structured lattice of water molecules (ice cube) placed in a warmer environment. Molecules at the surface gaining kinetic energy, breaking free from the lattice and becoming disordered liquid water, with heat arrows flowing into the ice.

Damped Pendulum Swing: A pendulum swinging, with small red arrows indicating air resistance opposing motion and a glowing red spot at the pivot representing frictional heating, causing the amplitude to visibly decrease over time.

Heat Pump/Refrigerator Irreversibility: A schematic of a heat pump. Show heat transfer from cold reservoir to working fluid (requiring work input) and then to hot reservoir, highlighting the finite temperature differences (e.g., refrigerant slightly colder than cold reservoir, slightly hotter than hot reservoir) that make it irreversible.

Entropy as Disorder: An animation starting with ordered objects (e.g., neatly stacked bricks) and then showing them falling and scattering into a disordered pile, illustrating the natural tendency towards increased entropy.

Thermal Equilibrium of Liquids: Two containers, one with hot (red) water particles, one with cold (blue) water particles. They are poured into a third container, and the particles quickly mix and average out to a uniform temperature (purple).

Rust Formation: Iron atoms forming a crystalline structure. Oxygen and water molecules interacting with the surface, breaking down the ordered iron structure and forming amorphous, flaky iron oxide (rust) with a visible change in texture and color.

Turbulent Fluid Dissipation: A simulation of fluid flow, starting with large, coherent eddies. These eddies break down into smaller and smaller vortices, ultimately showing the kinetic energy dissipating into random molecular motion (heat) at the smallest scales due to viscosity.

Infinitesimal Equilibrium in Reversible Expansion: A gas in a cylinder with a piston, showing a very small weight being removed, the piston moving by dV, and internal/external pressures remaining almost identical.

Reversible Isothermal Expansion: Heat & Work on PV: Animation of a gas expanding isothermally, with heat arrows entering from a reservoir and the evolving shaded area under the PV curve representing work.

Reversible Adiabatic Expansion: Temperature Drop & Work on PV: Animation of a gas expanding adiabatically in an insulated cylinder, showing a thermometer indicating decreasing temperature and the work area on the PV curve.

Heat Transfer Through Infinitesimal Temperature Steps: A system's temperature changing from T1 to T2, showing it exchanging heat with an infinite series of reservoirs, each dT apart, ensuring a reversible heat transfer.
Carnot Cycle Step 1: Isothermal Expansion (Q_H Absorption): Detailed animation of the piston moving out, gas expanding, heat Q_H being absorbed from the hot reservoir, and work done.

Carnot Cycle Step 2: Adiabatic Expansion (Temperature Drop): Detailed animation of the piston continuing to move out, insulation applied, gas expanding further, and temperature dropping from T_H to T_C.
Carnot Cycle Step 3: Isothermal Compression (Q_C Rejection): Detailed animation of the piston moving in, gas compressing, heat Q_C being rejected to the cold reservoir, and work done on the system.

Carnot Cycle Step 4: Adiabatic Compression (Temperature Rise): Detailed animation of the piston continuing to move in, insulation applied, gas compressing further, and temperature rising from T_C back to T_H.
Full Carnot Cycle on PV Diagram with Net Work: The four steps traced on a PV diagram, clearly showing the enclosed area representing the net work output of the engine.
Full Carnot Cycle on T-S Diagram with Heat Transfer Areas: The four steps forming a rectangle on a T-S diagram, with areas representing Q_H (below T_H line) and Q_C (below T_C line).

Entropy Change for Reversible Isothermal Process (Area on T-S): A horizontal line on a T-S diagram representing an isothermal process, with the area under it (Q_rev) divided by T, visually representing the change in entropy.

Comparing Reversible and Irreversible Work (Piston with Friction): Side-by-side animation of a piston expanding: one smoothly (reversible-like) and another with friction, showing less work output for the irreversible case.
Reversible Heat Pump Operation (Reverse Carnot Cycle): An animation of the Carnot cycle running backward, showing external work input to transfer heat from a cold reservoir to a hot one.
The Concept of Reversible Work as Maximum Work: A visual comparison of work done by a system in various processes, highlighting that the reversible path yields the maximum possible work.

Path Independence of Entropy Change for Reversible Processes: Showing two different reversible paths between the same initial and final states on a T-S diagram, demonstrating that delta S is the same for both.

Initial setup: Displaying pivot, string (length L), and bob (mass m) at equilibrium.
Displacement: Animating the bob being pulled to an initial angle theta, showing the arc path.
Gravitational force vector: Drawing 'mg' acting vertically downwards from the bob's center.
Tension force vector: Drawing 'T' acting along the string towards the pivot.
Coordinate system rotation: Aligning axes with and perpendicular to the string for force resolution.
Resolving gravity (mg cos theta): Projecting 'mg' along the string, balancing tension.
Resolving gravity (mg sin theta): Projecting 'mg' tangential to the arc, highlighting as restoring force.
Newton's Second Law: Applying F = ma to the tangential direction, ma = -mg sin theta.
Small angle approximation: Visualizing sin(theta) approximately equals theta for small angles on a graph.
Substituting approximation: Replacing sin theta with theta in the equation of motion.
Arc length and angle relation: Visualizing s = L theta, with arc length 's', radius 'L', and angle 'theta'.
Substitution of theta = s/L: Replacing theta with s/L in the equation, yielding ma = -(mg/L)s.
Rearranging to SHM form: Manipulating the equation to show a = -(g/L)s, comparing to a = -omega^2 s.
Derivation of angular frequency: Equating (g/L) with omega^2 and solving for omega = sqrt(g/L).
Final time period formula: Substituting omega into T = 2pi/omega to obtain T = 2pi sqrt(L/g).
Natural Length of a Spring: Visualizing a spring at rest with its unstretched length marked.

Introduction to an Electric Dipole: Showing two equal and opposite charges (+q and -q) appearing, separated by distance 2a, with the dipole moment vector 'p' highlighted.
Defining the Observation Point in Polar Coordinates: Animating a point P(r, θ) in space relative to the dipole's center, illustrating 'r' and 'θ'.
Distances from Each Charge to the Observation Point: Drawing and labeling the vectors r+ (from +q to P) and r- (from -q to P).
Electric Potential Contribution from the Positive Charge: Displaying the formula V+ = kq/r+ and highlighting r+ on the diagram.
Electric Potential Contribution from the Negative Charge: Displaying the formula V- = k(-q)/r- and highlighting r- on the diagram.
Applying the Superposition Principle for Total Potential: Animating the scalar sum V_total = V+ + V-.
Geometric Approximation for Distances in the Far Field (r >> a): Visualizing the projection of 'a' onto 'r' to approximate r+ ≈ r - a cosθ and r- ≈ r + a cosθ.
Algebraic Manipulation of (1/r+ - 1/r-): Step-by-step animation of substituting approximations and simplifying the term using binomial expansion or common denominator.
Introduction and Substitution of Dipole Moment 'p': Clearly defining p = q(2a) and animating its substitution into the potential expression.
Final Electric Potential Formula for an Electric Dipole: Displaying the derived formula V = (1/(4πε₀)) * (p cosθ / r²).
Equipotential Lines for an Electric Dipole (2D Cross-section): Animating the generation of various characteristic equipotential lines around the dipole.
Potential Along the Dipole Axis (θ = 0 and θ = π): Animating a point moving along the axis, showing how cosθ simplifies and the potential changes.
Potential Along the Equatorial Plane (θ = π/2): Animating a point moving along the equatorial plane, demonstrating where cosθ becomes zero, resulting in zero potential.
3D Visualization of Equipotential Surfaces: Animating a rotating 3D view of the dipole and several equipotential surfaces to convey their spatial form.
Visualizing an Electric Dipole: Showing two point charges (+q and -q) separated by distance 'd', and the resulting dipole moment vector 'p' pointing from -q to +q.
Uniform Electric Field Representation: Parallel and equally spaced electric field lines, indicating a constant magnitude and direction.
Dipole Placement in Uniform Field: An electric dipole positioned at an arbitrary initial angle (theta) relative to a uniform electric field.
Forces on Dipole Charges: Vector representation of the equal and opposite forces (F = qE and F = -qE) acting on the +q and -q charges of the dipole respectively.
Net Force and Torque Visualization: Demonstrating that the net force on the dipole in a uniform field is zero, while illustrating the couple of forces creating a net torque.
Torque Vector Calculation (p x E): Visualizing the cross product of the dipole moment vector 'p' and the electric field vector 'E' to show the direction and magnitude of the torque.
Dipole Rotation under Torque: Animating the rotation of the dipole from an initial angle towards alignment with the electric field due to the applied torque.
Work Done by Electric Field: Representing the infinitesimal work (dW = tau d(theta)) done by the electric field as the dipole rotates through a small angle.
Integral for Potential Energy: Visualizing the integration of torque with respect to angle to derive the change in potential energy from a reference point.
Reference Point for Zero Potential Energy: Explicitly showing the dipole configuration (theta = 90 degrees, p perpendicular to E) where its potential energy is defined as zero.
Potential Energy Formula Derivation: Step-by-step animation of the derivation of U = -p.E (or U = -pE cos(theta)) from the work done by the field.
Graph of Potential Energy vs. Angle: Plotting the potential energy of the dipole as a cosine function of the angle (theta) between p and E.
Stable Equilibrium State: Animating the dipole settling into its lowest potential energy state (theta = 0 degrees), perfectly aligned with the field.
Unstable Equilibrium State: Visualizing the dipole in its highest potential energy state (theta = 180 degrees), anti-aligned with the field, and demonstrating its instability.

Oscillation of Dipole: Showing the oscillatory motion of the dipole around its stable equilibrium position when released from an initial angle, illustrating potential and kinetic energy interconversion.
Parallel Plate Capacitor Construction: Animation showing two conductors separating with a dielectric material in between, forming a capacitor.
Charging Process Visualization: Electrons flowing from negative terminal to one plate, and from the other plate to the positive terminal of a battery, accumulating charge.
Electric Field Formation: Gradual emergence of uniform electric field lines between parallel plates as charge accumulates.
Voltage and Equipotential Lines: Visualization of potential difference building up across plates and the distribution of equipotential lines.
Capacitance Dependence on Plate Area: Animation showing how increasing plate area allows more charge storage for the same voltage.
Capacitance Dependence on Plate Separation: Animation showing how decreasing distance between plates increases capacitance, visualizing field strength changes.
Dielectric Material Effect: Animation showing a dielectric being inserted, its polarization, and the resulting reduction in the net electric field, leading to increased capacitance.
Energy Stored as Area under Q-V Graph: Visualizing the incremental work done (dQ*V) as the capacitor charges, forming an area under the Q-V curve.
Capacitors in Series Connection: Animation showing how charge is conserved across series capacitors and voltage divides, leading to an equivalent capacitance.
Capacitors in Parallel Connection: Animation showing how voltage is common across parallel capacitors and charges add up, leading to an equivalent capacitance.
RC Charging Curve Animation: Dynamic plot of charge (Q) and voltage (V) vs. time, alongside a circuit visualization showing current flow diminishing exponentially.
RC Discharging Curve Animation: Dynamic plot of charge (Q) and voltage (V) vs. time, alongside a circuit visualization showing current flow reversing and diminishing exponentially.
Dielectric Breakdown Simulation: Visualizing the electric field lines becoming too dense, leading to a sudden discharge path through the dielectric.
Variable Air Capacitor Operation: Animation showing rotating plates changing the effective overlapping area, thus varying capacitance.
Spherical Capacitor Field & Capacitance: Visualization of electric field lines between two concentric spherical shells and the derivation of its capacitance.

Net Electric Field Reduction: Superposition of the external and induced electric fields, demonstrating the overall reduction in the electric field strength inside the dielectric.

Polarization Vector (P): Macroscopic representation of the polarization vector as the average dipole moment per unit volume within the dielectric.

Dielectric Hysteresis: Illustrating the relationship between polarization (P) and electric field (E) for certain dielectrics, showing a hysteresis loop.
Frequency Dependence of Polarization Mechanisms: Animation showing different polarization mechanisms (electronic, ionic, orientational) responding at different frequencies of an oscillating E-field.

Power Dissipation in a Resistor: Visualizing a resistor heating up (e.g., glowing) as current flows through it, with energy converted from electrical to thermal.
Resistor Color Code Decoding: An animation showing a resistor with colored bands and then decoding them step-by-step into a resistance value with tolerance.
Impact of Wire Length on Resistance: Visualizing a longer wire increasing resistance, represented by more 'collisions' for electrons along an extended path.
Impact of Wire Cross-Sectional Area on Resistance: Visualizing a wider wire decreasing resistance, offering more 'paths' for electrons to flow.
Battery as a Voltage Source: Animation of a battery 'pumping' electrons from low potential to high potential, creating the voltage difference that drives current.
Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) in a Simple Loop: Animated demonstration of voltage rises and drops summing to zero around a closed loop in a basic circuit.

Voltage Equipotential Lines: Animated visualization of equipotential lines across the entire parallel segment, demonstrating uniform voltage drop across each capacitor.

Charge Flow and Accumulation: Animation of positive charge particles flowing from the source, splitting at the junction, and accumulating on the positive plates of each capacitor, with negative charges on opposite plates.
Individual Capacitor Charging Process: Simultaneous animation of charge density increasing on the plates of each capacitor over time, represented by growing plus/minus signs or color intensity.
Electric Field Lines within Each Capacitor: Visualization of electric field lines emerging from positive plates and terminating on negative plates for each individual capacitor in the parallel array.

Equivalent Capacitance by Area Summation: A conceptual animation where the plates of parallel capacitors are visually 'merged' to form a single equivalent capacitor with a larger effective plate area.
Total Charge as Sum of Individual Charges: Animated decomposition of the total charge from the source into individual charges on each capacitor (Q_total = Q1 + Q2 + ...), then their recombination.

Energy Stored in Parallel Capacitors: Visual representation of energy accumulation (e.g., glowing effect or expanding energy spheres) within each capacitor, demonstrating how total energy is the sum of individual energies.
Dynamic Plate Area Adjustment: Animating the change in effective plate area of one capacitor in the parallel combination, showing its individual capacitance changing while others remain fixed.

Dielectric Insertion and Effect: Animation of a dielectric slab sliding into one capacitor, showing the increase in its individual capacitance and corresponding charge accumulation (assuming constant voltage).
Current Splitting at Junction: Visualizing the total current (represented by moving dots) approaching the parallel junction and splitting proportionally to the capacitance of each branch.
Voltage Meter Across Each Capacitor: Animated display of a digital voltmeter connected across each parallel capacitor, consistently showing the same voltage reading as the source.

Charging/Discharging Cycle Visualization: A sequence showing parallel capacitors charging from a source, then disconnecting and discharging through a load resistor, with charge levels and current flow animated.

Capacitor Failure: Open Circuit: Animation demonstrating one capacitor becoming an open circuit (e.g., a break in its connection), showing that current no longer flows through that specific branch, but others continue to function.
Defining Point Charges and Separation: Visualizing two distinct spherical charges, q1 and q2, with their centers connected by a dynamic distance 'r' label.
Conceptualizing "Infinity" for Potential Energy: An animated sequence where charge q2 starts extremely far from q1 (off-screen) and gradually approaches, with 'r' decreasing.

Work Done Against Repulsion: A positive charge q2 being pushed towards a stationary positive q1, with an external force arrow overcoming the electric repulsive force arrow, and a "Work Done (W_ext)" counter accumulating.

Work Done by Attraction: A negative charge q2 approaching a stationary positive q1, showing the electric attractive force arrow pulling it, and a "Work Done (W_ext)" counter showing negative accumulation.
Derivation Visualization: Force to Potential Energy: A graph of F(r) vs. r for two charges, followed by shading the area under the curve from infinity to r, revealing the potential energy U(r).

Potential Energy Curve for Like Charges: An animated plot of U(r) = kq1q2/r (for q1,q2 > 0) versus 'r', showing the value increasing rapidly as r approaches zero and asymptotically approaching zero at infinity.

Potential Energy Curve for Opposite Charges: An animated plot of U(r) = kq1q2/r (for q1>0, q2<0) versus 'r', showing the value becoming more negative as r approaches zero and asymptotically approaching zero at infinity.

Electric Field Line Interaction (Repulsion): Dynamic visualization of electric field lines from two positive charges, bending away from each other, clearly showing the region of zero field between them.
Electric Field Line Interaction (Attraction): Dynamic visualization of electric field lines originating from a positive charge and terminating on a negative charge, illustrating the attractive force.
Equipotential Contours for Two Charges: Animated display of equipotential lines forming around two positive charges, then around a positive and a negative charge, highlighting their unique shapes.

Energy Conversion: Repulsive Release: Two positive charges initially held together, then released, showing their acceleration away from each other and the conversion of stored potential energy into kinetic energy.

Energy Conversion: Attractive Capture: Two opposite charges initially far apart, allowed to approach, showing their acceleration towards each other and the increasing (more negative) potential energy converting to kinetic energy.
Assembling the System (Step-by-Step): Visualizing q1 placed at origin (no work), then q2 brought from infinity to a distance 'r' from q1, showing the work done as the potential energy of the system.
Dependence on Charge Magnitudes: Dynamically adjusting the magnitudes of q1 and q2 (e.g., using sliders) and observing the instantaneous change in the calculated potential energy value displayed.

Dependence on Permittivity (Medium): Introducing a transparent block representing a dielectric medium (relative permittivity > 1) between the charges, showing how the electric field lines are reduced and the potential energy value changes.

Electron-Phonon Scattering Mechanism: Visualize electrons moving through a crystal lattice, with atoms vibrating. Show increased vibration amplitude (temperature) leading to more frequent and energetic electron collisions.
Mean Free Path Reduction in Metals: An animation showing the average distance an electron travels between collisions decreasing as the lattice vibration intensity (temperature) increases.

Band Gap Excitation in Intrinsic Semiconductors: An energy band diagram showing electrons moving from the valence band to the conduction band as thermal energy (temperature) increases, creating electron-hole pairs.
Comparative R vs T Curves: Metal vs. Semiconductor: Plotting the distinct resistance-temperature behaviors of a conductor (increasing R) and a semiconductor (decreasing R) on the same graph.
Superconductivity: Critical Temperature Transition: A dramatic graph illustrating resistance abruptly dropping to zero at a material's specific critical temperature (Tc).
Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) Thermistor Behavior: A graph showing the exponential decrease of resistance with increasing temperature for an NTC thermistor, explaining its sensitivity.

Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) Principle: Visualize a coiled platinum wire and explain how its precise and nearly linear resistance increase with temperature is used for accurate temperature sensing.

Matthiessen's Rule: Separating Resistivity Components: A graph showing total resistivity as the sum of a temperature-independent residual resistivity (due to impurities) and a temperature-dependent thermal resistivity (due to phonons).
Charge Carrier Concentration vs. Temperature in Semiconductors: A plot showing the exponential increase in the number of free electrons and holes as temperature rises in an intrinsic semiconductor.

Temperature Dependence of Doped Semiconductors (Extrinsic Behavior): Illustrate how donor/acceptor levels affect the R vs T curve, showing regions where impurity ionization or intrinsic behavior dominates.

Determining Activation Energy from an Arrhenius Plot: An animation demonstrating how the slope of a log(Resistance) vs 1/Temperature graph yields the activation energy or band gap for a semiconductor.

Effect of Impurities on Low-Temperature Resistance: Visualize static impurity atoms as fixed scattering centers, emphasizing their dominant role in resistance at very low temperatures where phonon scattering is minimal.

Visualizing the Impact of Thermal Expansion on Resistance: Show a resistor's physical dimensions (length and cross-sectional area) slightly changing with temperature, and how this affects R = rho*L/A.

Wheatstone Bridge for Thermistor-Based Temperature Measurement: An animated circuit diagram showing how a thermistor's changing resistance unbalances a Wheatstone bridge, producing a measurable output voltage proportional to temperature.

Visualizing Magnetic Flux: Animated representation of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, highlighting the area vector and field strength vector for calculating flux.
Changing Magnetic Flux via Area Change: A rectangular loop expanding and contracting within a uniform perpendicular magnetic field, showing the changing number of field lines through it.
Changing Magnetic Flux via Field Strength Change: A fixed loop with magnetic field lines appearing/disappearing or increasing/decreasing in density through it over time.
Changing Magnetic Flux via Orientation Change: A rectangular coil rotating about an axis perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, showing the angle between the area vector and B-field changing.

Faraday's Law - Graphical Relation: Simultaneous animation of a changing magnetic flux graph (
R
 

 
 
 vs. t) and the corresponding induced EMF graph (EMF vs. t), emphasizing the derivative.
Lenz's Law - Approaching Magnet: A bar magnet moving towards a coil, animating the induced magnetic field lines from the coil opposing the approaching magnet's field and the induced current.
Lenz's Law - Receding Magnet: A bar magnet moving away from a coil, animating the induced magnetic field lines from the coil trying to maintain the original flux and the induced current.
Motional EMF in a Sliding Rod: A conducting rod sliding on U-shaped rails in a perpendicular magnetic field, showing charge separation and the resulting induced current direction.
Motional EMF with a Rotating Rod: A conducting rod rotating about one end in a uniform magnetic field, showing the path of electrons and the potential difference developed across it.
Eddy Current Damping (Pendulum): A conducting plate pendulum swinging into and out of a strong magnetic field, illustrating the induced eddy currents and rapid damping.
Simple AC Generator Principle: A single rectangular coil rotating in a uniform magnetic field, visualizing the instantaneous induced EMF and current direction reversal.
Transformer Action (Primary-Secondary Flux Linkage): An oscillating current in a primary coil creating a changing magnetic flux that links to a secondary coil, inducing an EMF.
Magnetic Braking (Eddy Currents): A spinning conducting disk brought into a magnetic field, showing the induced eddy currents creating an opposing torque and slowing it down.
Effect of Number of Turns: Two coils with different numbers of turns experiencing the same rate of change of magnetic flux, visually comparing the magnitudes of the induced EMF.

Electromagnetic Damping (Falling Magnet in Tube): A bar magnet falling through a non-magnetic conducting tube (e.g., copper), showing its slowed descent due to induced eddy currents and opposing magnetic fields.
Bar Magnet and Poles: Visualizing a bar magnet with distinct North (N) and South (S) poles.
Magnetic Axis Definition: Drawing the line passing through the center of the magnet and extending through both poles.
Magnetic Dipole Moment Vector ("μ"): Representing the vector from the South pole to the North pole, labeled "μ".
Test Point 'P' on the Axis: Placing a point 'P' on the magnetic axis, outside the magnet, where the field is to be calculated.
Distances from Center and Poles: Labeling the distance from the magnet's center to P as 'r', and the half-length of the magnet as 'l'.
Field from North Pole (B_N): Showing the magnetic field vector at P due to the North pole, pointing away from N.
Field from South Pole (B_S): Showing the magnetic field vector at P due to the South pole, pointing towards S.
Relative Strengths of B_N and B_S: Visually comparing the magnitudes (lengths) of B_N and B_S based on their distances from P.
Vector Addition at Point P: Animating the superposition of B_N and B_S to find the resultant field at P.
Resultant Axial Magnetic Field (B_axial): Displaying the final resultant vector B_axial at P, pointing along the axis.

Direction of B_axial vs. 
μ: Highlighting that the resultant field B_axial is in the same direction as the magnetic dipole moment 
μ.
Distance Dependence: B_axial Shrinking: Animating point P moving further away along the axis, showing B_axial rapidly decreasing in magnitude.

Inverse Cube Law Indication: A text overlay indicating the approximate B_axial 
∝ 1/r^3 relationship for large distances 'r'.
Magnetic Field Lines Along Axis (Context): Briefly showing some surrounding magnetic field lines, particularly those extending along the axis from N to S.
Effect of Magnet Length (2l): Animating how changing the magnet's length '2l' affects the distances from poles to P, and consequently the magnitude of B_axial.

Visualization of Eddy Current Formation: A time-varying magnetic field (e.g., moving bar magnet) inducing circular current loops within a stationary conductive plate, showing current direction based on Lenz's law.

Eddy Current Braking Mechanism: A conductive plate moving through a static magnetic field, illustrating induced eddy currents creating an opposing magnetic field that slows the plate's motion, with force vectors.

Laminations Reducing Eddy Current Losses: Comparison of a solid iron core vs. a laminated iron core in an alternating magnetic field, demonstrating how laminations break up large eddy current loops, reducing current magnitude and heat.

Induction Heating Principle: A high-frequency alternating current in a coil creating a rapidly changing magnetic field, inducing intense eddy currents within a nearby conductive object, leading to rapid heating.

Eddy Current Damping of a Pendulum: A pendulum with a conductive plate swinging through a magnetic field, showing rapid amplitude reduction due to eddy current braking compared to a free-swinging pendulum.

Eddy Current Repulsion and Levitation (Dynamic): A strong magnet falling towards a thick conductive plate, illustrating induced eddy currents creating an opposing magnetic field that repels the magnet, slowing its descent.

Eddy Current Speedometer Operation: A rotating permanent magnet beneath a non-magnetic conductive cup, visualizing how the rotating field induces eddy currents, generating a torque that causes the cup to deflect against a spring.

Detection of Flaws using Eddy Currents (NDT): An eddy current probe scanning a conductive surface, showing how a subsurface crack or defect alters the path and magnitude of induced eddy currents, detectable by the probe.

Magnetic Shielding using Eddy Currents: A rapidly changing external magnetic field encountering a conductive enclosure, visualizing eddy currents on the surface creating an opposing magnetic field that cancels the external field inside.

Skin Effect Influence on Eddy Currents: An alternating current in a thick conductor, demonstrating how at higher frequencies, eddy currents (and thus current density) are concentrated near the surface.

Eddy Current Generation in a Rotating Disk (Arago's Disk): A rotating copper disk with a stationary magnet above it, visualizing the eddy currents induced in the disk by the changing flux, creating a torque on the magnet.

Eddy Current Heating in an Induction Cooktop: A simplified cross-section of an induction cooktop coil generating an alternating magnetic field, inducing eddy currents directly within the ferromagnetic base of a pot, heating the pot.

Eddy Current Losses in AC Generators/Motors: Illustrating unwanted eddy current loops forming within a solid iron core of a rotating armature, leading to energy dissipation and heating, and how laminations mitigate this.

Factors Affecting Eddy Current Magnitude: A visual comparison showing how changing magnetic field strength, speed of relative motion, or material conductivity/resistivity impacts the intensity of induced eddy currents.

Eddy Current Dangers: Unwanted Heating: A scenario where a rapidly changing magnetic field (e.g., from a high-power AC line) induces strong eddy currents in nearby unintended conductive structures, causing dangerous overheating.

Visualizing an Alternating Current (AC) Waveform: A sine wave representing current I(t) evolving over time, with labels for I_peak and period T.
Instantaneous Power in a Resistor (AC): Graphing P(t) = I(t)^2 * R, showing the double-frequency, always positive power curve alongside the current.
The Squaring Operation: I(t) to I(t)^2: Animating the transformation of the sinusoidal current waveform I(t) into its squared form I(t)^2.
Area Under the Squared Current Curve: Highlighting and calculating the area under one full cycle of the I(t)^2 graph.
Calculating the Mean (Average) of I(t)^2: Visualizing the process of finding the average height of the I(t)^2 curve over a period T, representing it as a horizontal line.
The Square Root Operation: From Mean(I^2) to I_RMS: Animating the final step of taking the square root of the average value, resulting in the I_RMS horizontal line.
Superimposing I_RMS on the Original AC Waveform: Drawing the horizontal line for I_RMS on the I(t) graph, showing its relation to I_peak.
Derivation: I_RMS = I_peak / sqrt(2) (Graphical Proof): Visually demonstrating how the I_RMS line's height relates to I_peak, perhaps by showing the sqrt(2) factor.
Heating Equivalence: AC vs. DC Current: Two identical resistors, one powered by AC (I_RMS) and one by DC (I_DC = I_RMS), showing equal thermal energy dissipated over time.
Visualizing V_RMS for Alternating Voltage: Applying the same squaring, averaging, and square-root process to a sinusoidal voltage waveform V(t) to find V_RMS.
Why Simple Average is Not Used: Graphically illustrating that the average value of a full cycle of AC current is zero, highlighting its inadequacy for power calculations.
RMS as the 'Effective' Value: A conceptual animation showing how I_RMS represents the DC current equivalent in terms of power delivery.
Power Calculation with RMS Values: Demonstrating P = V_RMS * I_RMS for a purely resistive circuit, emphasizing the use of effective values.
Peak-to-Peak Voltage/Current and RMS: Showing the relationship between V_peak-to-peak (or I_peak-to-peak) and V_RMS (or I_RMS).
Real-world Application: Household AC Voltage: Visualizing the stated 230V or 120V AC as V_RMS, and then showing its corresponding V_peak value (e.g., 120V RMS -> ~170V Peak).

Energy Exchange in a Capacitor: Visualizing the electric field building and discharging, showing energy being stored and returned to the source.
Complex Power Plane: Plotting the complex power vector S = P + jQ on the complex plane, showing its magnitude |S| and angle phi.
Power Factor Correction using Capacitors: Animation demonstrating how adding a parallel capacitor reduces the reactive power (Q) of an inductive load, shrinking the power triangle.
RMS Value Concept for Power: Comparing the heating effect of an AC current/voltage to an equivalent DC current/voltage, explaining the significance of RMS in power calculations.
Impact of Phase Angle on Real Power: Animating the cosine of the phase angle (cos phi) and its effect on the magnitude of real power (P = S cos phi) as phi changes.
Reactive Power as 'Sloshing' Energy: Visualizing the back-and-forth movement of energy between the source and reactive components without net work being done.
Defining key terms for spherical mirrors: pole, principal axis, center of curvature, radius of curvature, and focal point for both concave and convex mirrors.
Ray tracing rule 1: Parallel rays reflecting through the focal point (concave) or appearing to diverge from it (convex).
Ray tracing rule 2: Rays passing through/directed towards the focal point reflecting parallel to the principal axis.
Ray tracing rule 3: Rays passing through the center of curvature reflecting back along their original path.
Ray tracing rule 4: Rays incident at the pole reflecting symmetrically about the principal axis.
Image formation by a concave mirror: Object placed beyond the center of curvature, showing a real, inverted, and diminished image.
Image formation by a concave mirror: Object placed between the focal point and the pole, showing a virtual, erect, and magnified image.
Image formation by a convex mirror: General case for any object position, showing a virtual, erect, and diminished image.

Parallel Ray Reflection (Concave): An incident ray parallel to the principal axis striking a concave mirror and reflecting through its focal point.
